title: REACH - English Podcast
description: Improve your English listening and speaking skills with REACH — engaging podcast episodes covering everyday topics, culture, and practical conversations

episodes.title: How to Introduce Yourself in English
episodes.description: Learn natural ways to introduce yourself in both casual and formal situations

transcript.text: Hey everyone, welcome to the REACH English Podcast. I'm your host, and today we're going to talk about how to introduce yourself in English.
Whether you're meeting someone at a party, a job interview, or a new class — first impressions matter.
Let's start with the basics. The simplest way is: Hi, I'm [your name]. Nice to meet you.
In more formal situations, you might say: Good morning, my name is [full name]. It's a pleasure to meet you.
After the greeting, people usually ask: What do you do? or Where are you from?
A natural response could be: I'm a software engineer, or I work in marketing.
You can also add something personal: I'm originally from Vietnam but I've been living in London for three years.
Practice tip: try introducing yourself in front of a mirror. It helps build confidence.
Thanks for listening! See you in the next episode.

episodes.title: Ordering Food at a Restaurant
episodes.description: Useful phrases and vocabulary for dining out and ordering food in English

transcript.text: Welcome back to REACH English Podcast. Today's topic is ordering food at a restaurant.
Eating out is one of the most common situations where you need to speak English.
When you arrive, the host might say: Do you have a reservation? or How many in your party?
To order, you can say: I'd like the grilled chicken, please. Or: Can I have the pasta?
If you're not sure what to order, try asking: What do you recommend?
For drinks: I'll have a glass of water, please. Or: Could I get a coffee?
When you're finished: Could we have the bill, please? or Can I pay by card?
Remember, being polite is key. Use 'please', 'thank you', and 'excuse me' often.
That's all for today. Practice these phrases and you'll feel much more confident next time you eat out!

episodes.title: Talking About Your Daily Routine
episodes.description: Describe your daily activities using common English phrases and time expressions

transcript.text: Hey there! Welcome to REACH English Podcast. Today we're talking about daily routines.
Being able to describe your day is one of the first things you learn in English.
We use the simple present tense for habits. For example: I wake up at 7 AM every day.
Then: I brush my teeth, take a shower, and have breakfast.
For work or school: I leave the house at 8 and commute by bus.
In the evening: I usually cook dinner, watch some TV, and go to bed around 11.
Time expressions are important: in the morning, at noon, in the afternoon, in the evening, at night.
Frequency adverbs help too: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never.
Try describing your own daily routine using these words. See you next time!

episodes.title: Making Small Talk at Work
episodes.description: Learn how to have casual conversations with colleagues in a professional setting

transcript.text: Welcome to REACH English Podcast. Today's episode is all about small talk at work.
Small talk is casual conversation about unimportant things. But in reality, it's very important for building relationships.
Common topics include: the weather, weekends, hobbies, sports, and current events.
For example, on a Monday morning you might say: Hey, how was your weekend?
Or: Did you watch the game last night? That was an amazing match!
Near the coffee machine: This weather is crazy, right? I can't believe how hot it's been.
Avoid controversial topics like politics or religion — keep it light and friendly.
Active listening is also key. Nod, smile, and say things like: Oh really? That sounds great! Tell me more.
Practice makes perfect. Start with one small talk conversation today!

episodes.title: Traveling by Plane — Airport English
episodes.description: Essential English for navigating airports, checking in, and boarding your flight

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to REACH English Podcast. Today we're learning airport English!
Flying can be stressful, especially when English isn't your first language.
At check-in, you'll hear: May I see your passport and booking confirmation?
You might be asked: Would you like a window seat or an aisle seat?
At security: Please remove your laptop and any liquids from your bag.
At the gate: Flight BA245 to London is now boarding at gate 12.
On the plane: Could I have a blanket, please? And: Excuse me, I think that's my seat.
Key vocabulary: boarding pass, carry-on luggage, departure, arrival, layover, connecting flight.
A layover is a stop between flights. A connecting flight is the second flight you take.
That's it for today. Safe travels, and we'll see you in the next episode!

episodes.title: English for Job Interviews
episodes.description: Prepare for your next job interview with common questions and strong answers

transcript.text: Welcome to REACH English Podcast. Today we're preparing you for job interviews in English.
Job interviews can be nerve-wracking, but preparation is the key to success.
The most common first question is: Tell me about yourself.
Keep your answer professional and brief — about 1 to 2 minutes. Focus on your experience and skills.
Another common question: What are your strengths and weaknesses?
For strengths, give specific examples. For weaknesses, mention something you're working to improve.
Why do you want to work here? — Research the company beforehand and connect your goals to theirs.
At the end, they'll ask: Do you have any questions for us? Always say yes!
Good questions include: What does a typical day look like? or What are the growth opportunities?
Practice answering these questions out loud. Good luck with your interviews!

episodes.title: Understanding English Idioms
episodes.description: Common English idioms explained with examples so you can use them naturally

transcript.text: Hey everyone! Welcome back to REACH English Podcast. Today let's dive into English idioms.
An idiom is a phrase where the meaning is different from the individual words.
For example: It's raining cats and dogs. This doesn't mean animals are falling from the sky — it means it's raining very heavily.
Break the ice means to start a conversation in an awkward or new social situation.
Hit the nail on the head means to describe exactly what is causing a situation or problem.
A piece of cake means something is very easy. For example: The test was a piece of cake!
Bite the bullet means to decide to do something difficult that you've been avoiding.
Under the weather means feeling sick or unwell. I'm feeling a bit under the weather today.
The best way to learn idioms is to hear them in context. Listen to English podcasts, watch movies, and read books.
That wraps up today's episode. Keep learning, and see you next time!

episodes.title: Talking About Technology
episodes.description: Discuss gadgets, apps, and tech trends using modern English vocabulary

transcript.text: Welcome to REACH English Podcast! Today we're talking about technology in English.
Technology is everywhere, and being able to discuss it in English is essential in today's world.
Let's start with some basic vocabulary: smartphone, laptop, tablet, wearable, app, software, hardware.
When talking about phones, you might say: I just upgraded to the latest model. The camera is amazing.
For apps: I use this app for everything — it's so user-friendly. The interface is really intuitive.
Cloud storage means saving your files on the internet instead of on your device.
AI — artificial intelligence — is a hot topic. You might hear: AI is transforming the way we work.
Privacy concerns are also important: I'm worried about my data being collected by these companies.
Try discussing your favorite apps or gadgets in English with a friend. See you next time!

episodes.title: How to Give Opinions and Disagree Politely
episodes.description: Express your views and handle disagreements with respectful English phrases

transcript.text: Hello and welcome to REACH English Podcast. Today's topic: giving opinions and disagreeing politely.
Sharing your opinion is important, but how you say it matters just as much.
To give an opinion: In my opinion... I think that... I believe... From my point of view...
To agree: I totally agree. That's a good point. I feel the same way.
To disagree politely: I see your point, but... I'm not sure I agree. I understand what you're saying, however...
Avoid saying: You're wrong! or That's a terrible idea. These can sound rude.
Instead, use softening language: I might be wrong, but... Perhaps we could consider... Don't you think that...?
In a meeting, you could say: That's an interesting perspective. Could I offer an alternative view?
Practice these phrases in your next conversation. Being polite and clear is the goal!

episodes.title: English Pronunciation Tips
episodes.description: Common pronunciation mistakes and how to sound more natural in English

transcript.text: Welcome to REACH English Podcast! Today we have some pronunciation tips to help you sound more natural.
Pronunciation is one of the biggest challenges for English learners, but small improvements make a big difference.
Tip one: Focus on word stress. In English, we stress certain syllables more than others.
For example: it's phoTOgraphy, not PHOtography. And comPUter, not COMputer.
Tip two: Connected speech. Native speakers link words together. 'What are you' becomes 'Whatareyou' or even 'Whaddaya'.
Tip three: The schwa sound — the most common vowel sound in English. It sounds like 'uh'.
You hear it in words like: about, banana, support. The unstressed syllables use the schwa.
Tip four: Practice with shadowing. Listen to a native speaker and repeat immediately after them, copying their rhythm and intonation.
Tip five: Record yourself and compare with native speakers. You'll notice patterns to improve.
Remember, perfect pronunciation isn't the goal — being understood clearly is. Keep practicing!

episodes.title: Shopping in English
episodes.description: Learn how to ask about prices, sizes, and returns while shopping in English

transcript.text: Hey everyone, welcome to REACH English Podcast. Today we're going shopping — in English!
Whether you're buying clothes, groceries, or electronics, these phrases will help.
To ask about price: How much is this? or How much does this cost?
To ask about sizes: Do you have this in a medium? or Do you have this in a different color?
To try something on: Can I try this on? Where are the fitting rooms?
To ask about discounts: Is this on sale? Do you have any promotions?
To pay: I'll take this, please. Can I pay by card? Do you accept contactless?
For returns: I'd like to return this. Do you have the receipt? What's your return policy?
Useful vocabulary: receipt, refund, exchange, discount, clearance sale.
Practice these next time you go shopping. Happy shopping, and see you next time!

episodes.title: Describing People and Personality
episodes.description: Rich vocabulary for describing appearance and personality traits in English

transcript.text: Welcome to REACH English Podcast. Today we're learning how to describe people in English.
Describing people involves two things: appearance — how they look — and personality — how they behave.
For appearance: She's tall with curly brown hair. He has blue eyes and a beard.
More examples: She looks young for her age. He's well-built and athletic.
For personality, we have positive adjectives: friendly, generous, ambitious, reliable, easy-going.
And some negative ones: stubborn, impatient, arrogant, moody, selfish.
You can combine them: My sister is really outgoing and sociable, but she can be a bit impulsive sometimes.
A useful structure: He's the kind of person who always helps others. She's someone you can really count on.
Try describing someone you know using at least five adjectives. Great practice for building vocabulary!
